misreads.description: User reads Wiom as a mobile-data top-up product (e.g., interprets daily recharge + ₹23/day as a Jio/Airtel mobile pack)
misreads.why_dangerous: Already showing up in mid-funnel ads. Daily recharge is strongly associated with mobile internet. ₹23/day sounds like a mobile top-up. The 'ghar' anchor is what prevents it.
misreads.prevention: Explicit, unmistakable household / whole-home anchoring — visual, audio, or both. Never lead on flexibility without ghar anchor.
misreads.source: Context Note: 'The dangerous misunderstanding is mobile data product.' Brief §7.

misreads.description: User reads Wiom as broadband / fiber alternative, compares on speed / Mbps / monthly plan
misreads.why_dangerous: Pulls user back into the old evaluation frame. Wiom loses on speed/Mbps comparisons; the recharge advantage gets buried.
misreads.prevention: Avoid telecom category language. No speed wars, OTT bundles, fiber framing, Mbps claims.

misreads.description: User reads Wiom as low-cost WiFi — affordability without the category reframing
misreads.why_dangerous: Reduces Wiom to commodity. Sustained, this collapses the category back into broadband.
misreads.prevention: No 'cheapest', 'best value', 'sasta WiFi' framing. Affordability is reframed as 'manageable commitment + recharge control', not 'low price'.
misreads.source: Principles §6 NEVERs. Context Note language discipline.

weighting_rule: When weighing misread risk, mobile-data-product is weighted hardest. Broadband and cheap-WiFi are secondary. Do not weight all misreads equally.
